title: Advanced Prompting And Decomposition
summary: Break complex work into stages, use examples and structure more deliberately, and turn better prompting into better workflow design.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson sets the threshold for when the learner should stop using one-shot prompting and move into more structured prompt design.
lessons.title: When Simple Prompts Stop Working
lessons.subtitle: Complex work usually needs staged thinking, not longer prompts.
lessons.objective: Recognize when a task should be broken into parts instead of pushed through a single request.
lessons.useWhen: Complex work usually needs staged thinking, not longer prompts.
lessons.whyItMatters: As tasks get more complex, one-pass prompting often hides reasoning gaps, mixes goals, and creates difficult review work.

coreConcepts.title: Complexity Is A Workflow Problem
coreConcepts.text: When the task includes interpretation, tradeoffs, or multiple outputs, sequence matters more than clever wording.

coreConcepts.title: One Pass Creates Hidden Failure
coreConcepts.text: A single prompt can blur analysis, drafting, and recommendation into one opaque answer.

coreConcepts.title: Decompose By Decision Point
coreConcepts.text: Split the work where a human would naturally pause to review or redirect.

workedExample.scenario: A manager asks AI to analyze feedback, identify themes, recommend actions, and draft an executive memo in one prompt.
workedExample.approach: They split it into theme extraction, recommendation ranking, and memo drafting.
workedExample.result: Each step becomes easier to inspect and improve.
workedExample.verification: The manager checks the recommended actions against the extracted themes before asking for the memo.

doThisNow.task: Take one messy AI task and divide it into two or three stages.
steps: Decide what you will verify before moving on.

commonMistakes: Trying to solve workflow problems with longer prompts.
Combining analysis and recommendation in one opaque step.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: With the threshold clear, this lesson introduces examples as the first practical lever for improving consistency and format control.
lessons.title: Examples For Reasoning And Format
lessons.subtitle: Examples are useful when they teach the model how to think or how to shape output.
lessons.objective: Use examples more deliberately for classification, extraction, and consistent formatting.
lessons.useWhen: Examples are useful when they teach the model how to think or how to shape output.
lessons.whyItMatters: Well-chosen examples reduce ambiguity and improve repeatability on structured tasks.

coreConcepts.title: Examples Set The Pattern
coreConcepts.text: A short example can teach the model how to classify, prioritize, or structure output.

coreConcepts.title: Match The Example To The Task
coreConcepts.text: Use representative examples, not idealized ones that ignore real ambiguity.

coreConcepts.title: Explain The Lesson
coreConcepts.text: Tell the model what the example demonstrates so it copies the right thing.

workedExample.scenario: An analyst wants support tickets categorized consistently.
workedExample.approach: They provide three examples showing how to label root cause, severity, and next action.
workedExample.verification: They manually review a small sample to confirm the examples did not encode a bad rule.

artifact.label: Example Set
artifact.title: What a good example includes
items: Short note on why it is correct

doThisNow.task: Add two short examples to a prompt for a structured task you do often.
steps: Review whether the examples taught the right rule.

commonMistakes: Adding too many examples with no explanation.
Assuming examples remove the need for verification.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

resources.title: Anthropic multishot prompting guide
resources.note: Concrete guidance on using examples when consistent structure matters.

lessons.sequenceRationale: After examples, the learner needs a stronger scaffolding method for complex tasks that involve multiple requirements or sections.
lessons.title: Structured Prompts For Complex Work
lessons.subtitle: Headings, labels, and bounded sections help both the model and the reviewer.
lessons.objective: Use structured prompts to manage multi-part tasks more reliably.
lessons.useWhen: Headings, labels, and bounded sections help both the model and the reviewer.
lessons.whyItMatters: As prompts become longer, structure matters. Clear sections keep the request readable and make later edits easier.

coreConcepts.title: Label The Sections
coreConcepts.text: Separate goal, context, sources, constraints, and output format so nothing important is buried.

coreConcepts.title: Keep Each Section Purposeful
coreConcepts.text: Every section should change the answer. If it does not, remove it.

coreConcepts.title: Reuse The Shape
coreConcepts.text: Structured prompts are easier to turn into templates than free-form chat requests.

workedExample.scenario: A writer uses a long unstructured prompt for a proposal draft and keeps getting mixed results.
workedExample.approach: They reorganize the same information under clear headings with explicit source boundaries.
workedExample.result: The model follows the request more consistently and the prompt becomes reusable.
workedExample.verification: The writer checks whether the output reflects each section and whether any section can be shortened without loss.

doThisNow.task: Turn one messy real-world request into a structured prompt for an active document, memo, or report.
steps: Separate context, instructions, and output format into labeled sections.
Add one non-negotiable constraint and one success criterion.
Run the prompt on your real task and note what still needs review.

commonMistakes: Adding headings that do not affect the answer.
Packing too many tasks into one structured prompt anyway.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

resources.title: Anthropic XML tags prompting guide
resources.note: Useful for separating context, instructions, and examples in more complex prompts.

lessons.sequenceRationale: Only after structure is in place does prompt chaining make sense, because the learner can now break a workflow into reliable intermediate steps.
lessons.title: Prompt Chaining For Multi-Step Work
lessons.subtitle: Move from one answer to a managed sequence.
lessons.objective: Link multiple prompts into a workflow where each stage feeds the next.
lessons.useWhen: Move from one answer to a managed sequence.
lessons.whyItMatters: Prompt chains make complex work easier to inspect, improve, and reuse. They also map better to how real business tasks unfold.

coreConcepts.title: Define The Hand-Off
coreConcepts.text: Each stage should produce an output that is useful on its own and usable by the next stage.

coreConcepts.title: Insert Review Gates
coreConcepts.text: The chain should stop where a human must validate, choose, or redirect.

coreConcepts.title: Capture Intermediate Assets
coreConcepts.text: Save outlines, extracted themes, or ranked options because they can be reused later.

workedExample.scenario: A team lead wants AI help turning survey feedback into an action memo.
workedExample.approach: They chain prompts for extraction, theme grouping, recommendation drafting, and memo writing.
workedExample.result: The memo is more defensible because the upstream reasoning is visible.
workedExample.verification: They validate the extracted themes and ranking before generating the final memo.

doThisNow.task: Create a simple three-step prompt chain for a recurring task.
steps: Define the output of each stage.
Add one review gate before the final draft.

commonMistakes: Not defining what passes to the next stage.
Skipping the review gate because the final draft looks polished.

roleVariants.manager: Use AI to shorten prep, synthesis, and communication loops without removing judgment.
roleVariants.writer: Push for clearer source material, tighter briefs, and reusable language patterns.
roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

resources.title: Anthropic chain-of-thought guide
resources.note: Useful when a lesson covers decomposition and intermediate reasoning for complex tasks.

lessons.sequenceRationale: The final lesson packages the module into a reusable toolkit so the learner can choose the lightest prompt pattern that fits the job.
lessons.title: Your Advanced Prompt Toolkit
lessons.subtitle: Keep a small set of moves you can apply deliberately.
lessons.objective: Build a practical toolkit of advanced prompt moves and when to use each one.
lessons.useWhen: Keep a small set of moves you can apply deliberately.
lessons.whyItMatters: Advanced prompting is useful only when you know which move fits which problem.

coreConcepts.title: Use The Smallest Useful Move
coreConcepts.text: If a role and structure fix the problem, do not jump to a complex chain.

coreConcepts.title: Map Moves To Failure Modes
coreConcepts.text: Examples help with format consistency. Chains help with multi-step work. Critique prompts help with reasoning quality.

coreConcepts.title: Document What Works
coreConcepts.text: The toolkit becomes valuable when it is stored and reused, not when it lives only in memory.

workedExample.scenario: An operator keeps overcomplicating simple tasks with advanced prompt patterns.
workedExample.approach: They build a one-page toolkit listing failure modes and the smallest useful prompt move for each.
workedExample.result: Their prompting becomes simpler and more reliable.
workedExample.verification: They test the toolkit on three different tasks to make sure the guidance holds up.

doThisNow.task: Write a one-page toolkit mapping common failures to prompt moves.
steps: List three failure modes you see often.
Assign one prompt move to each.
Save one example for later reuse.

commonMistakes: Collecting prompt tricks with no workflow reason.
Not documenting when a move actually helped.

roleVariants.analyst: Separate analysis from storytelling so you can verify numbers before sharing narratives.
roleVariants.operator: Build repeatable handoff-ready outputs instead of one-off conversations.

outcomes: Decompose larger problems into better prompt workflows.
Use examples, structure, and chaining more deliberately.
nextModuleBridge: Next, bring those techniques into meetings, notes, and follow-up work.
